title: Design: Real-Time CAD Ingestion
scenario: A city's 911 Computer-Aided-Dispatch (CAD) system emits a continuous stream of call-for-service events — new calls, unit assignments, and status changes — that must feed a live command dashboard with under-2-second freshness and never drop an event. Steady-state volume is roughly 40 events per second, but a major incident can burst the stream to about 300 events per second for minutes at a time. You are designing the ingestion and serving layer end to end.

steps.label: Requirements
steps.prompt: Before choosing any technology, which pair of non-functional requirements most constrains this design?

choices.text: High throughput and schema flexibility above all — optimize for raw ingest speed and let the event shape evolve freely.
Durability (never drop an event) and low end-to-end latency (<2s freshness) — the design must guarantee both at once, even through a burst.
Strong consistency and exactly-once analytics — every downstream report must reconcile to the cent.
Cost efficiency and minimal operational footprint — keep the component count as low as possible.

steps.explanation: The brief names two hard guarantees: no event may be dropped (durability) and the dashboard must be under two seconds stale (latency). Holding both at once through a 7x burst is what forces a durable buffer plus a streaming path, and every later decision serves those two constraints. Throughput here is modest; cost and simplicity are real but secondary to a public-safety correctness bar.
hints: Reread the two promises the system makes to dispatchers and treat them as inviolable.
One requirement is about never losing data; the other is about how fresh the dashboard is.
Throughput is modest (tens to low hundreds per second); the binding constraints are correctness and freshness.

steps.label: Capacity
steps.prompt: You need a back-of-the-envelope capacity target for the buffer at the front door. Assume ~2 KB per event and a requirement to absorb a 10-minute major-incident burst without backpressuring producers. Which sizing is defensible?

choices.text: Provision for the 40 events/s average (~80 KB/s); bursts are rare and can be smoothed out later.
Provision for the ~300 events/s peak with headroom (~0.6 MB/s, ~360 MB buffered across a 10-minute burst) and partition the log to sustain that rate.
Provision for a hypothetical 10,000 events/s so capacity never has to be thought about again.
Skip sizing entirely — autoscaling will react to load as it arrives.

steps.explanation: A never-drop system is sized to its peak with headroom, not its comfortable average. The honest estimate multiplies peak rate by payload (300/s x 2 KB ≈ 0.6 MB/s) and then by burst duration (~360 MB over ten minutes), which fits easily in a partitioned durable log. Sizing to the average drops events exactly during the incident that creates the burst; over-provisioning 30x wastes money on a load two orders of magnitude off; and 'autoscaling will handle it' ignores that scaling lag is precisely when events get dropped.
hints: A never-drop system is sized to its peak, not its comfortable average.
Multiply peak events/s by bytes/event for throughput, then by burst seconds for buffered volume.
300/s x 2 KB is under 1 MB/s — the numbers are modest; provision for peak plus headroom.

steps.label: Ingest path
steps.prompt: How should events travel from the CAD producers to the dashboard so nothing is lost during a burst?

choices.text: Have CAD producers write directly to the dashboard's serving database over synchronous HTTP.
Publish events to a durable, partitioned, replayable log (e.g. Kafka/Kinesis); a stream processor consumes it and updates the read model.
Batch events into files and load them into the analytical warehouse every five minutes.
Push events into an in-memory queue on the dashboard server.

steps.explanation: A durable partitioned log decouples bursty producers from steady consumers, absorbs the 7x surge as buffered backlog instead of backpressure or loss, and — crucially — its replayability is what lets a crashed consumer recover without dropping events. A stream processor reading from it keeps the read model fresh within the latency budget. Writing producers straight to the serving DB couples them to its availability and offers no buffer; five-minute batch files blow the <2s freshness budget; an in-memory queue loses everything on restart.
hints: Producers are bursty and consumers are steady — you need something between them that can hold a backlog.
'Never drop an event' and 'recover after a crash' both point to durability and replay.
In-memory or direct-to-DB paths have no buffer and no replay; a log has both.

steps.label: Storage & serving
steps.prompt: The dashboard must show the current state of every active call in under two seconds. What serving design fits?

choices.text: Run the dashboard's queries directly against the raw event log, folding events into state on every page load.
Maintain a materialized read model (current state per call) updated by the stream processor, and separately land the raw events for analytics.
Point the dashboard at the analytical warehouse that the events eventually land in.
Cache the last N events in the browser and reconstruct call state client-side.

steps.explanation: The dashboard asks 'what is the state of each active call right now,' which is a materialized-view question: the stream processor folds the event stream into a current-state read model the dashboard reads in one fast lookup, while the raw events are also landed durably for the heavier analytical path. That is the operational/analytical split from the ingestion track. Re-folding the raw log per request won't meet <2s under load, the warehouse is built for batch analytics not live serving, and browser-side reconstruction can't be the source of truth.
hints: The dashboard wants current state, not history — that's a job for a maintained view, not a re-scan.
Recall the split between the low-latency operational path and the heavy analytical one.
Keep serving reads cheap by updating a read model as events arrive, and land raw events separately.

steps.label: Failure modes
steps.prompt: After a consumer restart, the log replays events it had already processed, and some events arrive twice. How do you stay correct?

choices.text: Trust that the log delivers each event exactly once and do nothing special.
Make processing idempotent with a stable event key — upsert current state, dedupe on event id — so replays and redeliveries can't double-count or corrupt state.
Add a downstream job that periodically scans for and deletes duplicate rows.
Switch the log to at-most-once delivery so duplicates never occur.

steps.explanation: Distributed logs deliver at-least-once, so redelivery is a certainty, not an edge case — the durable fix is idempotency: key each event stably and upsert, so applying the same event twice yields the same state. That makes replay-after-crash safe and is what lets you use the log's replay for recovery at all. Assuming exactly-once is wishful; periodic dedupe scans race the live dashboard and leave windows of wrong state; at-most-once 'solves' duplicates by dropping events, violating the never-drop rule.
hints: Real logs deliver at-least-once, so the same event arriving twice is guaranteed, not hypothetical.
The goal is that applying an event a second time changes nothing.
A stable event id plus upsert/dedupe beats both wishful 'exactly-once' and lossy 'at-most-once.'

steps.label: Retention/CJIS
steps.prompt: CAD records are Criminal Justice Information under a mandated retention schedule. How do you store the landed events for the long term?

choices.text: Keep every event in the hot serving store forever; storage is cheap enough.
Land raw events partitioned by time, tier older partitions to cheaper storage and drop them when the retention clock expires, with CJIS-compliant encryption at rest and immutable access-audit logs.
Purge events the moment a call is closed to minimize how much sensitive data is held.
Store the events unencrypted but restrict physical access to the building.

steps.explanation: This is the storage-track playbook applied to CJI: partition by time so the mandated retention cap becomes a partition drop instead of a mass delete, tier cold data down to control cost, and wrap it in CJIS controls — FIPS-validated encryption at rest, least-privilege access, and tamper-evident audit trails of every access. Hoarding everything hot ignores both cost and the retention schedule, purging on call-close destroys records you're legally required to retain, and physical security without encryption fails CJIS for data at rest.
hints: Partition by time so an expiry rule is a partition drop, not a billion-row delete.
CJI at rest means encryption, least-privilege access, and immutable audit logs — not physical security alone.
Retention is a mandated schedule: neither keep-forever nor purge-on-close satisfies it.
